result:
I want to write about the moment you realize the road you’re walking is only wide enough for one set of footprints, and they’re already yours. Not the cliché about seeing your own tracks behind you in supernatural sand, but the quieter, almost embarrassed recognition that no one else has ever walked here, and no one ever will. The trees lean in, not ominously, just curiously, the way librarians tilt when you open a book they’ve guarded for decades. You keep waiting for the soundtrack to rise—strings, kettle-drum, the usual cinematic con job—but instead you get the squeak of your own boot and the small wet click of your tongue because your mouth has gone dry and you can’t remember drinking water since yesterday. The sky looks like a badly erased pencil drawing: ghosts of clouds, half-hearted sun, the eraser crumbs of jet-trails. You think, absurdly, about the lunchbox you had in second grade, the one with the thermos that still smelled of ketchup even after you left it soaking all weekend in the sink. You hated ketchup, but your mother packed it anyway because your brother loved it and grocery budgets were Russian dolls: smaller versions inside smaller versions until the last doll was just empty wood. That’s the moment I’m talking about—when childhood thermoses and adult thirst and the impossibility of explaining any of this to another human all braid themselves into the same knot in your sternum.

We are told that language is a bridge, but every bridge I’ve ever trusted has charged a toll. So maybe it’s fair that the sentence I keep rehearsing—Hey, I think I’m lost—never quite makes it past the guard booth of my teeth. Instead I hum, a note I think is middle C but is probably closer to the drone of the universe clearing its throat. The trees don’t mind; they speak in chemicals anyway, pheromone telegrams about drought and beetles and the way a certain slant of light at 4:17 p.m. can feel like memory. I read once that forest networks are socialist: the older oaks funnel sugar to the stunted birch, no questions asked, no IOUs. I wonder what they’d make of my bootprints, of the plastic wrappers deep in my pockets—remnants of energy bars whose first ingredient is denial. Would they recognize the wrappers as the kindest thing I could manage: waste folded into itself, smaller and smaller, until it approximated obedience?

I keep walking because stopping feels like admitting something I’m not ready to confess. The trail, if it can still be called that, is a hesitation of moss between volcanic rocks. Every step is a negotiation with gravity and the small betrayals of cartilage. I think of my father at sixty, climbing Kilimanjaro because the guidebooks promised “every reasonably fit person can do it,” and how he came down three days later with stories not of summit glory but of a fellow trekker vomiting blood at 15,000 feet, the way the mountain turned alpine desert into a kind of moonlit battlefield where the enemy was your own hemoglobin. He spent the descent composing obituaries for strangers who survived. When he got home he gave away his boots and never walked farther than the mailbox without inventing a reason to rest halfway. I used to judge that surrender. Now I catalogue the contents of my own pack—two liters of water, a compass that may or may not point north, the silver cube of an emergency blanket that claims it can reflect back 90 percent of body heat—and I understand he wasn’t giving up; he was opting out of metaphors. Some mountains are only mountains; some questions only questions. Not every silence demands interpretation.

Still, I’d kill for a metaphor right now, something edible, like those thin candies wrapped in rice paper that dissolve on your tongue before you can figure out whether you were supposed to peel them first. I’d settle for a cliché: the reassurance that dawn follows night, that every cloud has a whatever, that the universe closes doors and opens windows, even though anyone who has ever moved a couch knows the window option is just landlord fan-fiction. What I get instead is the smell of crushed pine needles releasing a vapor that is simultaneously citrus and cat-piss, and the realization that I have been unconsciously counting my heartbeats since the last trail marker. The count is somewhere in the high eight hundreds; the units are ambiguous; the math is as useless as the motivational quote I once taped to my monitor: “You literally have 0 percent chance of making the shot you don’t take—W. Gretzky.” I left it there until the paper yellowed and a coworker asked why I hated myself enough to weaponize punctuation.

Here is what I know: the trail cannot be trusted. Topographers draw lines as if elevation gain were a gentle negotiation, a kind of geological diplomacy. They lie in two dimensions because shame is three. The contour I am following promises a rise of only two hundred feet over half a mile, numbers that sound like a children’s book: “200 was friends with 0.5 and they lived happily ever after.” Instead the earth folds itself into pleats so sharp they could slice bread. My thighs become a debate society specializing in recrimination. The air thins the way gossip does—by removing oxygen and replacing it with something metallic. Somewhere in the last scramble I scraped the knuckles of my right hand against granite, and now blood freckles the sleeve of my jacket, a private constellation. I lick the wound because bear manuals say scent invites death, but the copper taste is a referendum on every questionable decision since 1997.

Time behaves strangely when you are both lost and alone. Minutes inflate like cheap balloons, hours contract like muscles after a sprint. The sun lowers itself with arthritic dignity toward a ridge I cannot name. Shadows pool in the depressions of the land like dark water seeking its own level. I remember reading that depression is anger turned inward, but what is loneliness turned outward? It looks, I decide, like the space between ridge and sky right now: still, featureless, patient as a cat at a mouse hole. I have no signal, no map, no mirror. All I possess is momentum and the stories I barter with fear: if I reach the ridge before sunset, I’ll be able to see the trail again; if I can see the trail, I’ll find the lake; if I find the lake, I’ll remember the way back to the parking lot where my car waits like a loyal, slightly disappointed dog. The stories are paper cranes: delicate, useless, oddly beautiful when the light hits them.

The ridge arrives with no fanfare. It is not a triumph; it is simply the place the ground stops rising. I stand on a slab of granite scraped clean by glaciers and guilt, and the world tilts away in three directions: forest, more forest, and a sliver of silver that might be the lake or might be a mirror I dropped somewhere in 1989. The wind decides I need a talking-to. It combs through my hair, invents new part-lines, whispers that every plan I ever made was a children’s drawing taped to a refrigerator that has since been hauled to the dump. I expect panic. Instead I get clarity, the way a pond clears when the bottom settles: I am exactly where I have always been—on the thin crust of a sphere spinning at a thousand miles an hour, a sphere that does not care about my deadlines, my exes, my credit score, my unwritten novel. The realization is neither comfort nor terror; it is weather. I unzip my jacket, let the wind reach the sweat on my sternum, and for the first time all day the knot inside loosens by a single filament.

Dusk is a bruise spreading from the west. I need to choose: descend the scree field that glitters like broken chandeliers, or stay on the ridge and risk the night. I think of my father again, not on Kilimanjaro but in the backyard two weeks before he died, watering tomato plants with a hose that had more kinks than plot in a freshman short story. He looked up, said, “The trick isn’t knowing when to quit; it’s knowing what to quit chasing,” then sprayed his own foot and laughed like a man who had just won something small but decisive. I sit on the granite, unzip my pack, and eat the last energy bar, wrapper be damned. I fold the foil into a tiny square, smaller, smaller, until it could be a breadcrumb for an invisible bird. I leave it on the rock like a confession, shoulder the pack, and start downhill, not because I recognize the path, but because gravity owes me nothing and therefore can be trusted.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
